title: Charging a capacitor: the current is the slope of the charge
scenario: A capacitor $C = 1$ mF charges through a resistor $R = 1$ k$\Omega$ from a $V = 10$ V battery. The charge $Q(t)$ climbs toward $CV$ while the current $I(t)$ fades from $V/R$ to zero. Plotted as a stacked $Q$–$t$ over $I$–$t$, the slope of the charge *is* the current at every instant — the same slope↔value pivot as position and velocity, one domain over. Drag $R$ and watch the time constant $\tau = RC$@{em-rc-time-constant} stretch both curves.

assumptions.claim: An ideal series RC circuit: a constant-EMF battery, an ohmic resistor, and an ideal capacitor, with no lead resistance or dielectric leakage.
The capacitor starts uncharged, $Q(0) = 0$, so the initial current is the full $I_0 = V/R$ (the capacitor behaves momentarily like a wire).

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (a charging curve and a time constant)
steps.prose: The capacitor charges toward $Q_\infty = CV$, and the time constant $\tau = RC$ sets how fast: after one $\tau$ it is about $63\%$ charged, after $5\tau$ essentially full. You are handed the curve and the time constant as rules, with no account of *why* the approach is exponential or where $\tau = RC$ comes from.

steps.label: Kirchhoff's voltage law is a differential equation
steps.prose: Around the loop, the battery EMF equals the resistor drop $IR$ plus the capacitor voltage $Q/C$. Because the current is the rate of change of charge, $I = dQ/dt$, this is a first-order differential equation for $Q(t)$ — not an algebra equation.

steps.label: Solving it gives the charging curve; its slope is the current
steps.prose: The solution is the exponential approach to $CV$, with the time constant $\tau = RC$ falling straight out of the equation. Differentiating the charge gives the current: it starts at $V/R$ and decays. The current is the *slope* of the charge.

steps.label: Watch the two panels — I is the slope of Q
steps.prose: Drag $R$ or $V$: the charge $Q$ rises and levels off while the current $I$ decays, and at every instant the slope of the upper $Q$–$t$ curve is the value of the lower $I$–$t$ curve — the same relationship as position and velocity, in a circuit. The area under $I$ is the charge delivered. Raising $R$ stretches the time constant $RC$, slowing both.

proof.heading: The current is the slope of the charge, and the charge solves the RC equation — the kinematics slope↔value pivot, one domain over.
proof.detail: check $I = dQ/dt$; check $R\,dQ/dt + Q/C = V$; check $Q = \int_0^t I\,dt'$; $\tau = RC$ (63\% at $t=\tau$); steady state $Q\to CV$, $I\to 0$

checks.claim: The current is the slope of the charge: $I = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ — exactly as velocity is the slope of position.
The charge solves Kirchhoff's voltage law $V = IR + Q/C$, i.e. the RC equation $R\,\dfrac{dQ}{dt} + \dfrac{Q}{C} = V$.
The charge is the accumulated current — the area under $I$: $Q(t) = \int_0^t I\,dt'$.
The time constant is $\tau = RC$: at $t = \tau$ the capacitor is $1 - 1/e \approx 63\%$ charged, $Q(\tau) = CV(1 - e^{-1})$.
The steady state is full charge and no current: $Q \to CV$ and $I \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

misconception.claim: The capacitor charges at a steady rate, so the current stays constant while it fills up.
misconception.refuted_by: It does not. The current is *largest at the start* and decays exponentially as the capacitor fills: $I(t) = (V/R)\,e^{-t/RC}$. As charge accumulates, the capacitor's voltage $Q/C$ rises and opposes the battery, leaving a smaller voltage across the resistor and so a smaller current. Because the current is the slope of the charge, $I = dQ/dt$, a decaying current is exactly why the charging curve bends over and levels off instead of climbing in a straight line. Watch the panels: where $Q$ is steepest (at $t=0$) the $I$ curve is highest; where $Q$ flattens, $I$ approaches zero.

practice.asks: the time constant of the circuit
practice.prompt: The capacitor ($C = 1$ mF) charges through the resistor ($R = 1$ k$\Omega$). What is the time constant $\tau$?

choices.misconception: Used $R/C$ instead of the *product* $RC$. The time constant is $\tau = RC$ — ohms × farads gives seconds, while $R/C$ does not.
That's roughly the time to charge *fully* (about $5\tau$ reaches 99%). One time constant $\tau = RC$ is the time to reach 63%.

algebra_steps.prose: The capacitor charges toward $Q_\infty = CV$, and the time constant $\tau = RC$ sets how fast: after one $\tau$ it is about $63\%$ charged, after $5\tau$ essentially full. You are handed the curve and the time constant as rules, with no account of *why* the approach is exponential or where $\tau = RC$ comes from.

The area under $I$ is the charge delivered. Raising $R$ stretches the time constant $RC$, slowing both.

practice.asks: the current at the instant charging begins
practice.prompt: At $t = 0$, the instant the switch closes on the *uncharged* capacitor, what is the current?

choices.misconception: At $t=0$ the uncharged capacitor acts like a plain wire (a short circuit), so the current is at its *largest*, $I_0 = V/R$. It decays to zero only as $t\to\infty$.
Multiplied instead of divided. Ohm's law across the resistor gives $I_0 = V/R$, not $VR$.
